chunk_summary: A very low fat diet (<10%-15% of energy from fat) is critical for managing severe hypertriglyceridemia (>1,000 mg/dL) and reducing pancreatitis risk. Management includes addressing secondary causes, carbohydrate quality, and individualized protocols for familial chylomicronemia.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of very low fat diets in managing severe hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis risk.
Identify secondary drivers of hypertriglyceridemia and their management strategies.
Learn dietary guidelines, including fat and carbohydrate restrictions, for acute and long-term management.
Recognize the importance of individualized care in familial chylomicronemia and fat-soluble vitamin adequacy.
key_facts: Pancreatitis risk increases when triglycerides exceed ~1,000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L).
A very low fat diet is defined as <10%-15% of total energy from fat during acute management.
High refined carbohydrate and fructose intake can worsen hepatic triglyceride production.
MCT oil may be used in familial chylomicronemia as it bypasses chylomicron formation.
Long-term prevention strategies depend on the etiology, such as weight management or lifelong fat restriction.
Fat-soluble vitamin adequacy must be ensured during prolonged fat restriction.
reference_quote: Dietary fat restriction is the immediate risk-reduction strategy for severe hypertriglyceridemia because pancreatitis risk rises as triglycerides exceed ~1,000 mg/dL (11.3 mmol/L).
document_key_topics: Very low fat diet for severe hypertriglyceridemia
Long-term prevention and fat-soluble vitamin adequacy
